recipes.title: Feijoada
recipes.description: Brazil's national dish: a rich black bean stew slow-cooked with smoked pork, sausage, and beef, served with rice, farofa, and orange slices.
steps: Soak black beans in cold water overnight, then drain.
Soak carne seca in cold water for 4 hours, changing the water twice, then cut into chunks.
In a large pot, cover beans with fresh water, add bay leaves, and simmer for 1 hour until starting to soften.
Add carne seca, smoked ribs, sausage, and bacon. Continue simmering for 1 hour more.
In a skillet, sauté onion and garlic in olive oil until golden, then stir into the bean pot.
Cook another 30 minutes uncovered until the broth is thick and beans are creamy.
Season with salt and pepper. Serve with white rice, farofa, sautéed collard greens, and orange slices.

recipes.title: Pão de Queijo
recipes.description: Iconic Brazilian cheese bread from Minas Gerais — chewy, gluten-free puffs made with tapioca flour and Minas cheese.
steps: Preheat oven to 200°C (400°F) and line a baking sheet with parchment.
In a saucepan, combine milk, oil, and salt. Bring to a gentle boil.
Pour the hot liquid over the tapioca flour in a bowl and stir vigorously until a sticky paste forms.
Let rest for 10 minutes until cool enough to handle.
Add eggs one at a time, mixing well, then knead in the grated cheese until smooth.
Roll dough into golf-ball-sized rounds with oiled hands and place on the tray.
Bake for 20–25 minutes until puffed and lightly golden. Serve warm.

recipes.title: Moqueca
recipes.description: Bahian seafood stew simmered in coconut milk, tomatoes, peppers, and dendê (palm) oil, perfumed with cilantro and lime.
steps: Marinate fish with lime juice, garlic, salt, and pepper for 20 minutes.
In a wide clay or heavy pot, layer half the onions, peppers, and tomatoes on the bottom.
Place fish fillets on top, then layer the remaining vegetables over the fish.
Pour coconut milk and dendê oil over everything and scatter cilantro on top.
Cover and simmer over medium-low heat for 20–25 minutes until fish is cooked through.
Taste, adjust salt, and finish with more fresh cilantro.
Serve immediately with white rice and pirão (manioc flour porridge made from the broth).

recipes.title: Brigadeiro
recipes.description: Brazil's beloved chocolate fudge truffle made from condensed milk and cocoa, rolled in chocolate sprinkles.
steps: In a non-stick saucepan, combine condensed milk, cocoa powder, and butter over medium-low heat.
Stir constantly with a wooden spoon for 10–12 minutes.
The mixture is ready when you tilt the pan and the brigadeiro slides off cleanly without leaving residue.
Pour onto a buttered plate and let cool to room temperature, then refrigerate 30 minutes.
With buttered hands, roll the mixture into small balls about 2 cm in diameter.
Roll each ball in chocolate sprinkles to coat completely.
Place in mini paper cups and serve at room temperature.

recipes.title: Coxinha
recipes.description: Teardrop-shaped fried pastries filled with shredded chicken and cream cheese, coated in breadcrumbs.
steps: Poach chicken breast in broth for 20 minutes, then shred finely. Reserve 500 ml of the broth.
Sauté onion in butter until soft, add shredded chicken and cream cheese, season, and chill.
In a pot, bring reserved broth and milk to a boil with a pinch of salt.
Add flour all at once and stir vigorously over heat until a smooth dough forms and pulls from the pan.
Cool dough until handleable, then take a tablespoon, flatten in your palm, fill with chicken, and shape into a teardrop.
Dip each coxinha in beaten egg, then breadcrumbs to coat fully.
Deep fry at 175°C in batches until golden brown, about 4 minutes. Drain on paper towels and serve hot.

recipes.title: Acarajé
recipes.description: Bahian black-eyed pea fritters fried in dendê oil, traditionally split open and stuffed with vatapá and shrimp.
steps: Soak black-eyed peas in cold water for 8 hours, then rub between your hands to remove the skins.
Rinse repeatedly until skins float off and only the white peas remain.
Drain and blend peas with chopped onion and salt to a thick, fluffy paste — beat by hand for 5 minutes to incorporate air.
Heat dendê oil in a deep pan to about 175°C.
Using two soup spoons, scoop oval portions of paste and slide gently into the oil.
Fry for about 5 minutes, turning, until deep orange and crisp on the outside.
Drain on paper towels, slice open, and stuff with vatapá, caruru, and dried shrimp.

recipes.title: Vatapá
recipes.description: Bahian creamy paste of bread, shrimp, coconut milk, peanuts, and dendê oil — served with acarajé or rice.
steps: Soak bread in milk until softened, about 15 minutes.
Blend dried shrimp, peanuts, cashews, onion, garlic, and ginger to a fine paste.
Heat dendê oil in a heavy pot and sauté the nut-shrimp paste for 3 minutes.
Add the soaked bread mixture and coconut milk, stirring constantly over low heat.
Cook 15 minutes until thick, smooth, and creamy, stirring to prevent sticking.
Add fresh shrimp and cook 5 more minutes until pink and just cooked through.
Stir in cilantro, season with salt, and serve warm with rice or as filling for acarajé.

recipes.title: Picanha
recipes.description: Brazil's prized cut of top sirloin cap, salt-crusted and grilled over charcoal until charred outside and rosy within.
steps: Bring picanha to room temperature 30 minutes before grilling, fat cap intact.
Score the fat cap in a diamond pattern, being careful not to cut into the meat.
Cut the picanha against the grain into thick C-shaped steaks (about 4 cm wide).
Generously coat each steak with coarse rock salt on all sides.
Skewer each steak in a U-shape, fat side up, on a long metal skewer.
Grill over hot charcoal, fat side up first for 5 minutes, turning every few minutes for 12–15 minutes total.
Brush off excess salt, slice thinly against the grain, and serve with farofa and chimichurri.

recipes.title: Farofa
recipes.description: Toasted cassava flour mixture seasoned with bacon, onions, and eggs — Brazil's classic side for grilled meats.
steps: In a large skillet, render bacon over medium heat until crisp. Remove and reserve, leaving fat in pan.
Add butter to the bacon fat, then sauté onion until translucent and garlic until fragrant.
Pour in beaten eggs and scramble lightly until just set.
Reduce heat to low and gradually stir in the cassava flour, mixing constantly so it absorbs the fat evenly.
Toast the flour mixture for 5–7 minutes until golden and fragrant, stirring frequently.
Return crispy bacon to the pan, add scallions, and season with salt.
Serve warm as a side dish for feijoada, picanha, or any grilled meat.

recipes.title: Empadão de Frango
recipes.description: Large Brazilian chicken pot pie with buttery crust and creamy chicken filling laced with hearts of palm and olives.
steps: Make pastry: pulse flour, butter, salt, yolks, and ice water until dough forms. Chill 30 minutes.
Sauté onion and garlic, add chicken, tomato sauce, hearts of palm, and olives. Simmer 10 minutes.
Stir in cream cheese, season, and let cool to room temperature.
Roll out two-thirds of the dough and line a 24 cm pie pan, pressing into the corners.
Fill with chicken mixture, then roll out remaining dough and lay over the top, sealing edges.
Brush with beaten egg and prick the top to vent.
Bake at 180°C (350°F) for 40 minutes until deeply golden. Rest 10 minutes before slicing.

recipes.title: Pamonha
recipes.description: Sweet or savory fresh corn dumplings wrapped in their own husks and boiled — a classic from central Brazil.
steps: Carefully strip the husks from the corn cobs in whole pieces, then trim and reserve the largest leaves.
Cut the kernels off the cobs and blend with milk, sugar, butter, and salt until smooth but textured.
Form pouches with two overlapping husks, folding the bottom closed.
Spoon the corn paste into each pouch, fold over, and tie firmly with twine.
Bring a large pot of salted water to a boil and gently lower the parcels in.
Boil for 50–60 minutes, keeping the parcels submerged with a plate.
Drain, untie, and serve hot in their husks. Enjoy with butter or cheese.

recipes.title: Bobó de Camarão
recipes.description: Bahian shrimp and cassava cream stew flavored with coconut milk, tomatoes, and dendê oil.
steps: Marinate shrimp in lime juice, half the garlic, and salt for 15 minutes.
Boil cassava chunks in salted water for 20 minutes until very soft. Drain, reserving some cooking liquid.
Blend cooked cassava with coconut milk and a little cooking liquid into a smooth, thick cream.
In a large pot, heat dendê oil and sauté onion, remaining garlic, peppers, and tomatoes for 8 minutes.
Stir in the cassava-coconut cream and simmer 10 minutes, adjusting consistency with cooking liquid.
Add shrimp and cook 4–5 minutes until pink and just cooked.
Finish with cilantro, season with salt, and serve over white rice.

recipes.title: Pastel
recipes.description: Crisp deep-fried Brazilian street pastries with thin dough and assorted fillings like cheese, beef, or hearts of palm.
steps: Mix flour, salt, oil, cachaça, and warm water until a smooth dough forms. Knead 5 minutes, rest 30 minutes.
Brown ground beef with onion and garlic, season, stir in olives, and let cool.
Roll dough as thin as possible (almost see-through) on a floured surface.
Cut into 12 cm squares, place 1 tbsp of filling in the center of each.
Fold over to form rectangles, pressing edges and sealing with a fork or pastry wheel.
Heat oil to 180°C and fry pastéis 1–2 at a time for about 90 seconds per side, basting with oil to puff them.
Drain on paper towels and serve immediately with hot sauce or sugarcane juice.

recipes.title: Caruru
recipes.description: Bahian okra stew with shrimp, cashews, peanuts, and dendê oil — sacred dish in Candomblé tradition.
steps: Toss okra with lime juice and salt to reduce sliminess; rest 10 minutes.
Grind dried shrimp, peanuts, and cashews into a coarse powder.
Heat dendê oil and sauté onion, garlic, and ginger for 3 minutes.
Add okra and cook over medium heat, stirring constantly, for about 10 minutes.
Stir in the ground nut-shrimp mixture and 250 ml water, then simmer 10 minutes.
Add fresh shrimp and cook 4 minutes more until pink and just cooked.
Finish with cilantro, taste, and serve with rice or alongside acarajé.

recipes.title: Mandioca Frita
recipes.description: Crispy fried cassava sticks — Brazil's beloved bar snack, golden outside and fluffy inside.
steps: Cut peeled cassava into 8 cm sticks, removing the woody central core.
Place in a large pot, cover with cold salted water, and bring to a boil.
Simmer 15–20 minutes until tender enough to pierce with a fork but not falling apart.
Drain carefully and let cool completely — cassava can absorb oil if still warm.
Heat oil to 180°C in a deep pan.
Fry cassava sticks in batches for 4–5 minutes until golden and crisp.
Drain on paper towels, sprinkle with coarse salt, and serve immediately with garlic mayo or hot sauce.

recipes.title: Tropeiro
recipes.description: Hearty bean and cassava-flour dish from Minas Gerais with sausage, bacon, eggs, and collard greens.

ingredients.name: cooked pinto or carioca beans, drained

steps: In a large skillet, render bacon until crisp, then add sausage and brown. Remove and reserve.
In the rendered fat, sauté onion until soft and garlic until fragrant.
Pour in beaten eggs and scramble until just set, breaking into small pieces.
Add drained beans and cooked meats, stirring gently to combine.
Reduce heat and gradually stir in cassava flour, toasting for 5 minutes until everything is coated.
In a separate pan with a little olive oil, quickly wilt collard greens with a pinch of salt.
Stir greens and scallions into the tropeiro and serve immediately.
